title: The Sign to Ahaz
text: Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign. Behold, the young woman shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel.
summary: The prophet Isaiah delivers a sign to King Ahaz during the Syro-Ephraimite crisis of 735 BCE. A young woman will bear a child named Immanuel as a sign that the immediate threat will pass.
ntClaim: Matthew 1:22-23 quotes this as a prophecy of the virgin birth of Jesus, using the Greek Septuagint's 'parthenos' (virgin) rather than the Hebrew 'almah' (young woman).

title: Out of Egypt
text: When Israel was a child, I loved him, and out of Egypt I called my son.
summary: The prophet Hosea recalls the national memory of the Exodus, describing Israel as God's beloved child called out of slavery in Egypt.
ntClaim: Matthew 2:15 applies this line to the infant Jesus returning from Egypt, treating a historical reflection about Israel as a prediction about one person.

title: Psalm of Distress
text: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? All who see me mock me; they hurl insults, shaking their heads. They pierce my hands and my feet. They divide my garments among them and cast lots for my clothing.
summary: A psalm of individual lament. The poet cries out in abandonment, describes mockery and physical suffering in vivid imagery, and ultimately declares trust in deliverance.
ntClaim: The Gospels weave multiple details from this psalm into the crucifixion narrative: Jesus quotes verse 1 from the cross (Mark 15:34), soldiers cast lots for his clothing (John 19:24), and bystanders mock him (Matthew 27:39).

title: From Bethlehem
text: But you, Bethlehem Ephrathah, though you are small among the clans of Judah, out of you will come for me one who will be ruler over Israel, whose origins are from of old, from ancient times.
summary: The prophet Micah names the small clan district of Bethlehem Ephrathah as the ancestral origin of a coming ruler, linking future hope to the ancient Davidic lineage.
ntClaim: Matthew 2:5-6 cites this verse to explain why Jesus must be born in Bethlehem. The original text emphasizes dynastic clan origin rather than literal birthplace.

title: Seventy Weeks
text: Seventy weeks are decreed for your people and your holy city: to finish transgression, to put an end to sin, and to bring in everlasting righteousness. An anointed one shall be cut off and shall have nothing.
summary: A symbolic chronological vision spanning from a decree to rebuild Jerusalem through the cutting off of an anointed leader to the desolation of the sanctuary.
ntClaim: Christian tradition calculates the seventy weeks as a countdown to Jesus. Historians identify the anointed one cut off as the high priest Onias III, murdered circa 170 BCE, and the desolation as Antiochus IV's desecration in 167 BCE.

title: A Child Is Born
text: For to us a child is born, to us a son is given, and the government will be on his shoulders. And he will be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace.
summary: A royal hymn celebrating the birth or coronation of a Judean king. The titles are throne names in the ancient Near Eastern tradition, declaring the new king's divine mandate.
ntClaim: Luke 1:32-33 echoes these titles for Jesus. In the original context, these are coronation throne names, likely for King Hezekiah, following ancient Near Eastern convention where kings received divine titles at enthronement.

title: The Servant's Suffering
text: He was despised and rejected by mankind, a man of suffering and familiar with pain. He was pierced for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him.
summary: Part of the Servant Songs in Second Isaiah, written during the Babylonian exile. The suffering servant is most likely a personification of Israel in exile, bearing the consequences of national calamity.
ntClaim: Acts 8:32-35 and much of Christian theology identify the servant as Jesus. In context, Isaiah 40-55 repeatedly identifies the servant as Israel (Isaiah 41:8, 44:1, 49:3), and the suffering describes the exile experience.

title: Rachel's Lament
text: A voice is heard in Ramah, mourning and great weeping, Rachel weeping for her children and refusing to be comforted, because they are no more.
summary: Jeremiah describes the matriarch Rachel weeping as the people of Israel are marched into exile past her traditional burial place at Ramah after the Babylonian conquest.
ntClaim: Matthew 2:17-18 applies this to Herod's massacre of infants in Bethlehem. The original passage is about the Babylonian deportation, and the very next verse (Jeremiah 31:16-17) promises the exiles will return — a promise about the exile, not about a future massacre.

title: Swords into Plowshares
text: He shall judge between the nations and shall decide disputes for many peoples; and they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks; nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore.
summary: The messianic age requires literal, universal peace — the end of all war between nations, not merely a spiritual metaphor.
ntClaim: Christianity redefined 'peace' as an inner spiritual state. But the text describes geopolitical reality: no more armies, no more war. Two thousand years later, wars have not ceased.

title: The Scattered Flock
text: Fear not, for I am with you; I will bring your offspring from the east, and from the west I will gather you. I will say to the north, Give up, and to the south, Do not withhold; bring my sons from afar and my daughters from the end of the earth.
summary: The messiah will physically gather all Jewish exiles from every direction back to the land of Israel.
ntClaim: Rather than gathering the Jewish diaspora, the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE scattered the Jewish population further. The ingathering never occurred.

title: The Everlasting Sanctuary
text: I will make a covenant of peace with them. It shall be an everlasting covenant with them. And I will set my sanctuary in their midst forevermore. My dwelling place shall be with them, and I will be their God, and they shall be my people.
summary: The messiah will establish God's sanctuary — the Temple — permanently. It will stand forever, not be destroyed again.
ntClaim: The Second Temple was destroyed by Rome in 70 CE — forty years after Jesus. No third temple has been built. Jesus said 'Destroy this temple and I will raise it in three days,' but the Gospel of John reinterprets this as his body, not the actual building.

title: All Shall Know
text: They shall not hurt or destroy in all my holy mountain; for the earth shall be full of the knowledge of the Lord as the waters cover the sea.
summary: In the messianic age, knowledge of God will be universal and self-evident — no one will need to teach it because everyone will already know.
ntClaim: After two millennia, Christianity accounts for roughly a third of the world's population. Billions follow other religions or none at all. The universal, self-evident knowledge described here has not materialized.

title: The Broken Line
text: Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord, when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch, and he shall reign as king and deal wisely, and shall execute justice and righteousness in the land.
summary: The messiah must be a biological descendant of King David through the paternal line — tribal affiliation in ancient Israel passed from father to son.
ntClaim: Matthew and Luke both trace Jesus' lineage through Joseph to David — but their genealogies contradict each other. More critically, if the virgin birth is true, Joseph is not Jesus' biological father, which breaks the very patrilineal descent both genealogies try to establish.
